1: Ṭôb pat ḥărēbāh wĕšalwāh-bāh = 'Better a dry morsel with quietness/peace in it' — pat ḥărēbāh = a dry/crumbled morsel (the opposite of fat, moist food). Mibayit mālē' zibeḥê-rîb = 'than a house full of sacrificial feasts with strife' — sacrificial feasts (zibeḥê) represent the height of abundance and religious occasion; the contrast is between austere peace and sumptuous conflict.
2: 'Ebed maśkîl = 'A wise servant' who rules over a disgraceful son — social mobility through wisdom: the slave who exercises wisdom surpasses the birth-right of the fool-son. Ûbĕtôk 'aḥîm yḥaliq naḥalāh = 'and among brothers he will share in the inheritance.' Wisdom overturns normal social hierarchy.
3: Maṣrēp lakkesep wĕkûr lazzāhāb = 'A crucible/refining pot for silver and a furnace for gold' — maṣrēp (refining pot) and kûr (furnace/smelting furnace) are the metallurgical vessels; ûbōḥēn libbôt YHWH = 'but the tester of hearts is the LORD.' The divine smelting-test applies to the heart what the crucible applies to metal: YHWH's scrutiny purges and reveals what is genuinely valuable.
4: Mērēa' maqšîb 'al-śepat-'āwen = 'An evildoer listens to wicked lips' — the audience shapes the speaker: those who listen to wickedness are themselves wicked. Šeqer maqšîb 'al-lĕšôn hawwôt = 'a liar pays attention to a destructive tongue' — the mutual reinforcement of wickedness in speech-listening: you listen to what you are.
5: Lō'ēg lārāš ḥērēp 'ōśēhû = 'Whoever mocks the poor insults his Maker' ('ōśēhû = the one who made him) — the same creation-theology as 14:31. Śāmēaḥ lĕ'ēd lō' yinnāqeh = 'the one who rejoices at calamity will not go unpunished' — the prohibition on gloating extends to all suffering; malice in the face of others' disasters is an active moral failing.
6: 'ăṭeret zĕqênîm bĕnê bānîm = 'The crown of the elderly is grandchildren' — the multigenerational structure of wisdom-transmission: grandchildren are the elderly person's crown because they represent the continuation of the legacy. Wĕtipʾeret bānîm 'ăbôtām = 'and the glory of children is their fathers' — the bidirectional nature of generational honor: children bring honor to fathers; fathers are the crown of children.
7: Lō' nā'wāh lĕnābāl śepat-yeter = 'Not fitting/appropriate for a fool is excellent speech' — nābāl (fool/knave) is the morally deficient person. The irony: fine speech in a fool's mouth is as incongruous as lying lips in a nobleman's. The implication is that speech quality must match character to be authentic; eloquent lies are more dangerous than clumsy truths.
8: 'Eben-ḥēn haššōḥad bĕ'ênê bĕ'ālāyw = 'A charm-stone/magical stone (ḥēn) is the bribe in the eyes of its owner' — the bribe is described as a jewel or lucky stone that seems to work like magic. Bĕkol-'ăšer yipneh yaśkîl = 'wherever it turns it prospers.' This is social observation without explicit moral comment — the effectiveness of bribery is noted; the condemnation is implicit in the broader context.
9: Mĕkasseh-peša' mĕbaqqēš 'ahăbāh = 'Covering/overlooking a transgression seeks love' — kāsāh (cover) is a positive act here: not sweeping sin under the rug but choosing not to expose or repeat an offense, in service of relationship. The contrast: šôneh bĕdābār maphrid allûp = 'but repeating a matter separates close friends' — allûp again (v28); the intimate friendship most vulnerable to gossip.
10: Tērad gĕ'ārāh bĕmêbîn = 'A rebuke sinks deeper into a discerning person' than a hundred blows into a fool. The receptivity to correction is the diagnostic of wisdom — not how much punishment is required, but how much truth is received. The discerning person is changed by a single word; the fool is unaffected by violence.
11: Ak-merî yĕbaqqēš-rā' = 'Only/nothing but rebellion does the evil man seek' — merî (rebellion/contumacy) as the single definitive characteristic. Ûmal'āk 'akzārî yĕšullaḥ-bô = 'and a cruel messenger will be sent against him' — divine retribution in the form of a harsh emissary; what you cultivate is what you receive.
12: Pāgoš dōb šakkûl bĕ'îš = 'Meet a bereaved bear rather than a fool in his folly' — the bereaved bear (dōb šakkûl) robbed of her cubs is one of the ancient world's most dangerous animals; the fool's danger exceeds even this. The comparison calibrates the lethality of the fool: the bear's danger is situational; the fool's is chronic and incorrigible.
13: Mēšîb rā'āh taḥat ṭôbāh = 'One who returns evil for good' — the treachery of repaying good with evil is the deepest form of relational betrayal. Lō'-tāmûš rā'āh mibbêtô = 'evil will not depart from his house' — the principle of sowing and reaping (Gal 6:7 echoes this) applies to treachery: the household of the betrayer becomes the dwelling-place of the evil he returned.
14: Pōṭēr mayim rēšît mādon = 'The beginning of strife is like letting out water' — pōṭēr (let out, release) of water describes the first crack in a dam: once the water is released, the whole structure collapses. The MKT 'start of conflict is like a crack letting out water' captures this vividly. Ûlibnê hithraqqēaḥ hārîb = 'so abandon the quarrel before it breaks open' — wisdom acts at the first sign of fracture, not after the flood.
15: Maṣdîq rāšā' ûmarší'a ṣaddîq = 'Acquitting the wicked and condemning the righteous' — both are abominations to YHWH. The judicial perversion is double: falsely clearing the guilty and falsely condemning the innocent. This is the central prophetic concern (Isa 5:20,23; Amos 5:7; Mic 3:11) placed in the wisdom frame: miscarriage of justice is categorically intolerable to YHWH.
16: Lāmmāh-zeh mĕḥîr bĕyad-kĕsîl liqnôt ḥokmāh = 'Why is there a price in the hand of a fool to buy wisdom, when he has no heart for it?' — the irony is economic: the fool might have the purchasing power but lacks the capacity to receive what wisdom offers. Wĕlēb 'āyin = 'no heart' — not absence of desire but absence of the necessary cognitive-moral organ for wisdom acquisition.
17: 'Āhēb 'ohēb bĕkol-'ēt = 'A friend loves at all times' — the 'at all times' (bĕkol-'ēt) is the mark of genuine friendship: not situational but constant. Wĕ'āḥ lĕṣārāh yiwwālēd = 'and a brother is born for times of adversity.' The friend as 'all-times' lover and the brother as 'adversity-born' companion: friendship transcends circumstance while brotherhood is revealed by it.
18: Ādam ḥăsar-lēb tôqēa' kāp = 'A man lacking sense gives a pledge/strikes hands in surety' — echoing 6:1-5; the surety-pledge is the sign of deficient practical wisdom. 'ōrēb 'ărubāh lipnê rē'ēhû = 'gives surety in the presence of his neighbor' — the public nature of the commitment makes it even more binding and more reckless.
19: 'Ōhēb peša' = 'One who loves transgression loves strife' — the love of sin produces communal damage as a natural output. Magbîah petaḥô mĕbaqqēš-šāber = 'one who raises his gate high seeks destruction' — the 'high gate' is probably an image of arrogance/ostentation that provokes conflict; 'seeking destruction' describes the inevitable result of proud self-assertion.
20: 'Iqēš-lēb lō'-yimṣā'-ṭôb = 'The crooked-of-heart does not find good' — 'iqēš (crooked) is the same term as 16:28 (tahpukôt); fundamental distortion produces a life where good is inaccessible. Wĕnehpāk bilšônô yippôl bĕrā'āh = 'and the one perverted in his tongue falls into evil' — speech and character are inseparable in Proverbs; crooked speech is both symptom and cause of moral downfall.
21: 'To have a fool for a son brings grief' (lĕtûgāh hôledô) — the parental perspective: the father's grief at a foolish son is deep precisely because it is personal, not abstract. Wĕlō' yiśmaḥ 'ăbî nābāl = 'and the father of a fool does not rejoice' — the double statement intensifies: not just sadness but absence of joy. Compare the prodigal son's father's joy at the son's return (Luke 15:24) as the inversion of this portrait.
22: Lēb śāmēaḥ yêṭib gēhāh = 'A joyful heart (śāmēaḥ) makes good/is a good healing/medicine' — gēhāh (healing/cure) is the medical term; the joyful heart is therapeutic. Wĕrûaḥ nĕkē'āh tĕyaббēš-gārem = 'but a crushed/stricken spirit dries the bones' — the psychosomatic connection: inner spirit directly affects bodily health. Modern medical understanding of stress physiology resonates with this.
23: Šōḥad miḥêq rāšā' yiqqāḥ = 'The wicked takes a bribe from the bosom' — ḥêq (bosom/fold) is where valuables were hidden in ancient clothing; the bribe is taken from its hiding place. Lĕhaṭṭôt 'orḥôt mišpāṭ = 'to pervert the paths of justice' — the specific damage of bribery: not merely corruption but the structural distortion of the justice system itself.
24: Pĕnê-mêbîn ḥokmāh = 'Wisdom is before/in the face of the discerning' — the discerning person is always looking at wisdom, always attending to it. Wĕ'ênê kĕsîl biqṣēh-'āreṣ = 'but the eyes of a fool go to the ends of the earth' — the fool's gaze is scattered and unfocused; wisdom and folly are distinguished by where the eyes habitually rest.
25: 'A foolish son is grief to his father and bitterness to her who bore him' (kā'as lĕ'ābîw) — the maternal-paternal grief is distinct: kā'as (vexation/anger) for the father; memer (bitterness) for the mother. The deeply personal nature of parental grief at a foolish child is not abstract moralism but the real relational cost of folly.
26: Gam 'ănôš laṣṣaddîq lō'-ṭôb = 'Even to punish the righteous is not good' — the fundamental injustice of punishing the innocent. Lehakkôt nĕdîbîm 'al-yōšer = 'to strike nobles for what is right' — a secondary injustice: punishing the honorable for their integrity, which is a form of persecution of virtue.
27: Ḥōśēk 'ămārāyw dô'ēa' da'at = 'The one who restrains his words knows knowledge' — ḥāśak (restrain/hold back) is the virtue of verbal restraint; the knowledgeable person recognizes that not every thought needs expression. Wĕqar-rûaḥ 'îš tĕbûnāh = 'and a cool spirit (qar-rûaḥ) is a person of understanding.' Cool-spirited is the antithesis of the hot-tempered person of 14:29; 16:32.
28: Gam 'ĕwîl maḥăriš ḥākām yēḥāšēb = 'Even a fool who keeps silent is counted wise' — the paradox: silence can simulate wisdom even in the foolish. 'Ōṭēm śĕpātāyw nābôn = 'the one who keeps his lips shut is discerning.' The implication is not that silence is always wisdom but that wisdom's primary visible marker — restrained speech — can be faked; genuine wisdom requires both the restraint and the inner reality.
